Please describe your research career to date
Career to date
My career experiences have reinforced my desire to pursue a career as a leading clinical academic in
Rheumatology. I completed my undergraduate medical studies and an intercalated degree at the University of
Cambridge, earning yearly academic prizes. My Foundation Training took place at the Countess of Chester
Hospital, followed by an MSc in Immunology at the University of Oxford. Subsequently, I secured an NIHR
Academic Clinical Fellowship (ACF) with run-through specialty training in Rheumatology. Currently, I am an ST5-
level trainee in the South London programme.
Research Experience
I developed a passion for research during my intercalated degree with a project investigating the effect of
propranolol on the reconsolidation of conditioned fear memories. This involved animal model work, laboratory work
and a literature review, resulting in a second-author publication in Neuroscience.
I was first drawn to a career in rheumatology during my Fo

secarb__001_b.text:
undation Training. During this time, I audited the local
management of inflammatory arthritis and led a pilot trial on the efficacy of dietary intervention for gastroparesis in
patients with systemic sclerosis. I presented these projects at the British Society for Rheumatology (BSR) annual
conference. I also furthered my skills in critical appraisal by co-authoring a review article on post-transplantation
diabetes mellitus.
My MSc in Immunology enhanced my understanding of basic science and critical appraisal. During this time, I
undertook a research project investigating the effect of talarozole on inflammatory signalling in chondrocytes. This
involved laboratory work (chondrocyte isolation, culture and stimulation and Western blotting) and data analysis.
My primary NIHR ACF research project was a feasibility trial investigating the effect of different biological
medications on pain outcomes in participants with rheumatoid arthritis. I consented participants for recruitment,
conducted study visits and provided supervision to the research nurses and research fellows; enhancing my
leadership and management skills. I also performed data analysis, using SPSS and GraphPad. Although
par

secarb__001_c.text:
ticipant recruitment was lower than expected due to the COVID-19 pandemic, the work was published and I
was proud to be a co-author.
The COVID-19 pandemic redirected my ACF research to studying the clinical outcomes of patients with
autoimmune conditions who developed COVID-19. During this work, I submitted applications for ethical approval,
conducted trial visits, performed laboratory work (including PBMC isolation, ELISAs and ELISpots) and analysed
trial data. I presented my findings at national and international conferences.
During my ACF, I authored three literature reviews, two case reports and a book chapter, improving my skills in
scientific writing. I was the first author of a book introducing clinical research concepts to medical professionals. I
wrote chapters on statistics, clinical study design and ethical and governance considerations in research. I also
delivered an oral presentation at the BSR Case-Based Conference and completed the PGCert in Healthcare
Research Skills and Methods at St George’s University with modules in statistics, research methods and critical
appraisal.
I am a current member of the BSR working group for the ANCA-associated vasculitis guidelines.

secarb__001_d.text:
I have attended
working group meetings, completed a systematic literature review, written consensus statements and drafted the
final document. Throughout this process, I have worked closely with academic experts at leading UK centres,
highlighting the importance of networking and collaboration in research.
Research Experience in Sarcoidosis
My rheumatology training sparked an interest in multi-system conditions, particularly sarcoidosis. Whilst caring for
patients with sarcoidosis, I became aware of the paucity of research into this condition, when compared to other
inflammatory diseases. Despite being associated with significant morbidity and mortality, I noticed a significant
lack of epidemiological and trial data in this area.
Working with Dr Bechman and Professor Galloway, I performed a systematic review of biological medications in
sarcoidosis. Only nine RCTs were identified, highlighting an unmet research need. This work was presented at the
European Alliance of Associations for Rheumatology (EULAR) Congress and I have written a manuscript that is
currently under submission. More recently, we have used population-wide primary care data to describe the
epidemiology of sarcoido

secarb__001_e.text:
sis in England, highlighting a four-fold increase in prevalence over the last two decades.
We have submitted this work to the American College of Rheumatology conference, and are currently drafting a
manuscript for submission for publication.
To foster collaboration with charity representatives, I am working with Sarcoidosis UK to write patient information
leaflets. I am also organising a national multi-disciplinary sarcoidosis study day at King’s College London.
Management Experience
As an ST4 trainee, I served as Royal College of Physicians (RCP) Associate Clinical Tutor, liaising between
medical trainees and the RCP College Tutor and organising regular postgraduate teaching, including a mock exam
for medical trainees. As the current Chief Rheumatology Registrar at Guy’s and St Thomas’ Hospital, I attend
departmental meetings, organise medical student teaching, and oversee the registrar rota.
Teaching Experience
During medical school, I supervised junior students in clinical examination and pathology. In Foundation Training, I
designed and organised simulated on-call sessions for final-year medical students and presented the feedback as
posters at National and International Confe

secarb__001_f.text:
rences.
Throughout training, I have developed my expertise in medical education; authoring rheumatology-themed
questions for nationwide educational platforms and delivering a pan-London session on “Rheumatology Spot
Diagnoses” that was attended by 315 doctors. I continue to teach regularly.
Impact of Fellowship
Sarcoidosis is an under-researched condition associated with significant mortality and morbidity. If awarded an
NIHR doctoral research fellowship, I aim to develop methods to identify patients with sarcoidosis at high risk of
severe disease. My research will provide objective clinical and radiological measurements of disease activity,
guiding clinical trial design on a global scale. Improved identification of patients with severe disease will guide
referral to specialist centres and prescription of second-line immunosuppressive therapies, improving patient
quality of life and reducing healthcare burdens.
This fellowship would enable me to pursue my career goal of becoming a research leader in the field of
sarcoidosis. The skills that I gain during this research training programme will put me in a strong position to submit
a successful application for an NIHR Clinical Lecture

secarb__001_g.text:
ship and to continue my work in sarcoidosis.

secpesor__001_a.text:
Background
Sarcoidosis is a disease that causes lumps called “granulomas” to form in the body. These granulomas cause
inflammation and organ damage. Sarcoidosis is rare, but it is important to understand because it can cause death.
The problem
Sarcoidosis affects patients in very different ways. In some patients, sarcoidosis is very mild and gets better on its
own. In other patients, sarcoidosis can be deadly. It is very difficult to predict which patients will develop severe
disease. It’s also hard to predict which patients will die from their disease. Therefore, doctors struggle to know
which patients need treatment. There haven’t been many clinical trials in sarcoidosis and we don’t know which
treatment is best.
Sarcoidosis can affect any part of the body. PET-CT is a special type of medical scan which can find affected
organs and guide treatment. Doctors at different hospitals use PET-CT scans in different ways. If we knew how
best to use PET-CT scans, they could tell us which patients are at the greatest risk of severe disease.
Aim of my research
The aim of my research is to identify which patients are at risk of severe sarcoidosis. We want to know how factors
such as age, sex
secpesor__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_b.text:
, and ethnicity affect the risk of severe disease. We also want to know how the pattern of disease
on PET-CT scans affects the patients’ risk.
My research will help:
 Find patients who need early treatment. This will reduce the risk of organ damage and death.
Identify patients who qualify for clinical trials of new treatments. This will drive further research into

sarcoidosis, with the hope of finding new effective medications.
 Target resources to groups of people who need them the most. This will help NHS managers to allocate
healthcare services effectively.
Design and methods
I will divide my research into three parts.
Phase 1: We will collect evidence from medical records in GP practices. We will do this using a secure
system called OpenSAFELY. We will look at patients with sarcoidosis and compare those with mild and severe
disease. We will look at their age, sex, ethnicity. We will also consider their other health conditions and pattern of
sarcoidosis. We will determine whether there are any factors that increase the risk of death or organ damage.
Phase 2: We will train an artificial intelligence (AI) model. It will measure disease in the heart and lungs on PET-
CT scans.
secpesor__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_c.text:
We will test the model to make sure that it is accurate.
Phase 3: We will look at patients with sarcoidosis over a one-year follow-up period. All patients will have a PET-
CT at baseline. They will do this as part of their routine clinical care. We will use the AI model to find PET-CT scan
features that predict severe disease one year later.
Patient and public involvement
I have worked with the charity Sarcoidosis UK to speak to different patients. I conducted interviews and a focus
group to understand the impact of sarcoidosis. Six patient representatives will continue to guide the project. They
will help to design the study and analyse the results. Patient input will ensure that research focuses on benefiting
patients.
Dissemination
My patient panel will help ensure that my results reach as many people as possible.
Many approaches will be used:
 Plain English summaries of all publications.
 Newsletters of outputs, which can be disseminated through charities including Sarcoidosis UK.
 Social media outputs, including Facebook posts.
 Public engagement events, organised in conjunction with Sarcoidosis UK.

secsa__001_a.text:
Research question
Which patients with sarcoidosis are most at risk of severe disease?
Background
Sarcoidosis is a multisystem and clinically heterogeneous condition. It is associated with high mortality rates,
primarily due to respiratory or cardiac involvement. The prevalence of sarcoidosis is rising, with our preliminary
data showing a four-fold increase over the last 20 years in the UK.
Corticosteroids are first-line therapy in sarcoidosis but are associated with significant side effects and relapse
when tapered. There is limited evidence to guide which patients will benefit from second or third-line
immunosuppressive medications. Treatment is therefore non-standardised and often based on expert opinion.
In sarcoidosis, affected body organs show a particular affinity for the 18F-FDG tracer used in PET-CT, meaning
that this imaging modality has a unique ability to quantify organ involvement. Despite this, objective measures of
sarcoidosis activity on PET-CT do not exist.
There is a need to develop robust methods to identify patients at risk of severe forms of disease. This will enable
appropriate referral to specialist centres, early consideration of second-line treatments and st

secsa__001_b.text:
andardisation of
recruitment into clinical trials.
Aims and objectives
My research plan has 3 phases:
1. To identify demographic and clinical risk factors for adverse outcomes in patients with sarcoidosis.
2. To develop a deep-learning model capable of quantifying sarcoidosis activity in the heart and lungs on
PET-CT.
3. To identify PET-CT correlates of disease activity and future disease outcomes.
Methods (timelines of delivery)
Phase 1 (0-12 months): Using the nationwide OpenSAFELY platform, I will analyse clinical and demographic risk
factors for adverse outcomes, including end-organ damage, hospitalisation and death. Regression models will be
used to correlate predictors of interest with adverse outcomes.
Phase 2 (6-18 months): Using PET-CT performed in patients with confirmed sarcoidosis, I will develop a novel
deep-learning algorithm to quantify and characterise disease burden and pattern on PET-CT, focusing on uptake
in the heart and lungs.
Phase 3 (12-36 months): I will perform an observational trial in patients with biopsy-confirmed sarcoidosis with
PET-CT performed at baseline. Using the deep-learning algorithm developed in Phase 2, I will use regression
models to correla

secsa__001_c.text:
te disease burden with contemporary disease activity and clinical outcome at one year.
Patient representatives will be involved at all stages of study design and analysis, ensuring that my research
remains patient-focused.
Anticipated impact and dissemination
My research will allow non-expert clinicians to identify patients at risk of life-threatening sarcoidosis, who should be
considered for referral to specialist centres and treatment with second-line immunosuppressive medications. This
will improve the care of patients with sarcoidosis, reducing hospitalisations and death. My research will also
inform the design of clinical trials of immunosuppressive medications for sarcoidosis on a global scale. I will
disseminate my findings through publications, presentations and collaboration with colleagues from relevant
medical specialities e.g. cardiology and respiratory medicine. The expertise of my PPI panel will guide
dissemination to patients, carers and the wider public.

secdrp__001_a.text:
What is the problem being addressed?
Sarcoidosis is an inflammatory condition characterised by granuloma formation in organs, most commonly the
lungs and lymphatic system. Its clinical presentation can vary markedly, from mild and self-limiting, to severe
organ damage and death1. Sarcoidosis is associated with an increased risk of mortality, largely due to respiratory
or cardiac involvement2,3. It is a rare disease and therefore research into sarcoidosis is often neglected.
Throughout this application, I have worked closely with patients and gained an appreciation of the enormous
impact of a diagnosis of sarcoidosis on the individual. Not only is the condition associated with burdensome
symptoms, treatment options are limited and often lead to significant side effects. Our preliminary data shows that
about 22,000 people are living with sarcoidosis in England. These patients represent an underserved population in
urgent need of evidence-based improvements in their care.
Research into sarcoidosis is often underfunded due to a lack of interest from pharmaceutical companies due to
disease rarity. Corticosteroids remain the first-line therapeutic option but are associated with significa

secdrp__001_b.text:
nt side
effects including hypertension, diabetes, weight gain, osteoporosis, psychosis and infection4. Some patients with
sarcoidosis do not respond to corticosteroids and require second-line therapy e.g. methotrexate5. Second-line
immunosuppressive agents are effective treatments that can reduce steroid burden6. Although experts advocate
for earlier use of steroid-sparing agents, they are prescribed for less than half of treatment-requiring patients7.
The focus of my research proposal is to understand which patients are at the highest risk of developing severe
disease. This will allow the identification of a high-risk cohort of patients, who should be considered for second-line
immunosuppressive medications early. In addition to aiding clinical decisions, my work will drive objective methods
of clinical trial inclusion and data collection, improving the efficacy of randomised controlled trial (RCT) design in
sarcoidosis.
Why is this research important in terms of improving the health and wellbeing of the public and to patients
and health and care services?
This research will have a beneficial impact across a range of stakeholders:
Patients and carers
There is an enormous need to i

secdrp__001_c.text:
mprove the quality of life of patients with sarcoidosis. To explore the impact of the
diagnosis on patient well-being, I conducted nine interviews with patient representatives. These highlighted the
huge impact of sarcoidosis on overall health. Patients stated that their symptoms were often debilitating and that
treatments were unsatisfactory, sometimes resulting in intolerable side effects (two patients developed severe
mental health side effects secondary to high-dose steroids, including suicidality). The diagnosis of sarcoidosis was
often associated with significant anxiety due to the uncertainty surrounding the diagnosis, treatments and
prognosis. Patients felt that research into sarcoidosis was neglected and were enthusiastic to discuss research
priorities.
By identifying risk factors for severe disease, I hope to characterise a cohort of patients who should be initiated
onto second-line immunosuppressive medications earlier. This would reduce steroid-associated side effects,
improving quality of life. Patients at risk of severe disease could be recruited into clinical trials, driving the
development of novel and effective treatments.
Multi-disciplinary medical specialities
Sa

secdrp__001_d.text:
rcoidosis is managed by many medical specialities, including rheumatology, respiratory, cardiology, neurology,
ophthalmology and dermatology teams. Interviews with patient representatives highlighted the disjointed care that
patients with sarcoidosis often receive and the lack of interdisciplinary work in some cases.
My research will provide information on how different patterns of organ involvement predict outcomes and this will
guide us on how care should be joined up. My research will be relevant to guideline developers on the standards
of care for multi-system sarcoidosis. I will work with relevant societies, e.g. the British Thoracic Society, the British
Society of Rheumatology and the European Society of Cardiology, to disseminate my work and ensure that it
translates to improved patient outcomes.
Impacts on wider healthcare provision
By clarifying which patients will benefit from second-line immunosuppression, my research has the potential to
reduce steroid-related side effects, improve resource allocation, improve patient outcomes, reduce disability and
reduce costs for commissioners. As an example, our data estimates that 1.5% of patients with sarcoidosis go on to
require

secdrp__001_e.text:
home oxygen therapy. This is an expensive therapy and the total annual cost for home oxygen in the UK is
approximately £120 million8. Even small reductions in the number who require this treatment would significantly
benefit healthcare resource utilisation.
Identification of demographic predictors of severe sarcoidosis should allow appropriate targeting of healthcare
resources to those who are most in need. In the longer term, through the identification and treatment of patients
with an aggressive disease phenotype, I aim to reduce the burden of emergency department admissions and
hospitalisation of these patients. This will ease pressures on the already-stretched NHS services.
Review of the existing evidence - how does the existing literature support this proposal?
Sarcoidosis is associated with high mortality rates. About 5% of patients with sarcoidosis die from their
disease, and observational studies indicate that mortality rates are doubled compared to matched controls9, 10,11.
Currently, we lack methods to predict which patients will die from sarcoidosis.
Sarcoidosis is an under-researched condition and disproportionately affects groups that are under-
represented in research

secdrp__001_f.text:
studies, including people of Black ethnicity and those with socioeconomic
deprivation12,13,14,15. The lifetime risk for sarcoidosis is three times higher in African-American than in Caucasian
Americans (0.85% versus 2.4%)13. Furthermore, Afro-Caribbean ethnicity has been associated with organ-
prognosis16,17. research12.
threatening involvement and a poor These groups are under-represented in I will
address this throughout my research.
The prevalence, incidence and mortality rates from sarcoidosis are increasing over time10,11. Our
preliminary data, using Clinical Practice Research Datalink (CPRD), demonstrates a four-fold increase in the
prevalence of sarcoidosis over the last 20 years (Figure 1). There were an estimated 3,450 new diagnoses in
England in 2022 (Figure 2).
Newer imaging modalities are increasing our understanding of sarcoidosis and organ-threatening disease.
18F-FDG PET-CT is a functional imaging modality that is used in the diagnosis and monitoring of patients with
sarcoidosis (Figure 3). Despite its commonplace use, we do not have validated methods to quantify disease
severity on PET-CT18.
The optimal treatment of sarcoidosis is uncertain. Trials in sarcoidosis a

secdrp__001_g.text:
re limited by disease rarity,
heterogeneity and a lack of standardised trial outcome measures19. Current guidelines are based on expert
opinion and small uncontrolled trials9,20,21. Corticosteroids are the first-line treatment option but are associated with
significant toxicity; observational studies link an increased dose of corticosteroids with an increased number of
non-sarcoidosis-related emergency department visits, recurrent flares and higher mortality rates22,23,24. Steroid-
sparing agents are effective treatments but are underutilised, especially outside of tertiary units6.
There is an unmet need for better treatments in the management of sarcoidosis. Our systematic review
highlighted the lack of high-quality RCTs investigating the efficacy of second-line immunosuppressive agents in
sarcoidosis. As newer treatment options are emerging, there is a need to identify patients with severe disease who
are most likely to benefit from them.
My research aims to characterise risk factors for severe sarcoidosis. This work will identify patients who would
benefit most from second-line immunosuppressive therapies.
What is the research question/aims and objectives?
Research question:
Whi

secdrp__001_h.text:
ch patients with sarcoidosis are most at risk of severe disease?
Key aims and objectives
My key study phases, aims and objectives are:
Phase 1: Characterise the clinical and demographic predictors of adverse outcomes in patients with sarcoidosis
(0-12 months).
Phase 2: Develop a deep-learning model capable of localising and quantifying disease activity on 18F-FDG PET-
CT (6-18 months).
Phase 3: Correlate activity on PET-CT with contemporary disease activity and disease outcome (12-36 months)
Project Plan
Phase 1: Clinical and demographic predictors for poor outcomes (0-12 months)
Overview
Phase 1 is a nationwide cohort study comparing the rate of severe disease outcomes between patients with and
without predictors of interest.
Background
In American and Scandinavian populations, risk factors for poor prognosis in sarcoidosis include socio-economic
deprivation, obesity and Black ethnicity7,16,17. These have not been studied in a UK population, limiting our ability to
target healthcare resources and minimise inequalities in healthcare. This phase will investigate, characterise and
quantify the risk factors for severe disease on a patient and population-based level. This will enable i

secdrp__001_i.text:
mproved
identification of patients at risk of severe disease, improving patient management and healthcare policy planning.
Data Source
I will undertake analyses of sarcoidosis epidemiology using the OpenSAFELY Trusted Research Platform, a
secure nationally representative cohort of over 58 million patients, registered with general practices (GPs) in
England that use TPP and EMIS electronic health record software. This large dataset is well-suited for
epidemiological analyses on rare conditions, such as sarcoidosis, by increasing the likelihood of capturing a
sufficient number of individuals with the disease and enhancing the statistical power of the analyses. The use of
OpenSAFELY carries advantages over CPRD, a database that contains coded and anonymised data from GP
practices, which includes data from only 20% of the English population.
When using OpenSAFELY, the researcher develops analytical codes on dummy data. The analytic code is sent to
the live data environment and run securely on real patient data. The summary results are then reviewed by the
researcher. By blocking users from directly viewing the patient’s raw data, privacy risks are minimised and data
safety is optimised

secdrp__001_j.text:
. This has gained the trust of patient groups and is becoming the gold standard of
epidemiological data analysis. Another major advantage the use of OpenSAFELY platform is the pre-existing
linkage to the Office for National Statistics (ONS), Hospital Episode Statistics (HES) and Index of Multiple
Deprivation (IMD).
Methods
The following definitions will be used throughout this phase of the project:
Index diagnosis of sarcoidosis – A first-ever diagnostic code of sarcoidosis between 2004 and 2024, in people
aged 18 and above, permanently registered with a GP in England that uses TPP and EMIS electronic health
record software.
Severe disease - Disease resulting in hospitalisation, end-organ damage or mortality. Patients will also be
considered to have severe disease if they require treatment escalation or a cumulative lifetime prednisolone dose
greater than 500mg.
Predictors of severe disease - Demographic and clinical factors that may predict a severe disease outcome.
Outcomes of severe disease:
Outcomes are defined as events that occur at or after the time of diagnosis of sarcoidosis. They will include;
hospitalisation and end-organ damage, treatment escalation, corticosteroid burd

secdrp__001_k.text:
en and mortality.
Hospitalisation and end-organ damage
All-cause and sarcoidosis-related hospitalisation rates will be calculated using OpenSAFELY links to HES. Rates
will be expressed in crude and age-standardised forms.
Examples of markers of end-organ damage, stratified by organ system, are listed in Table 1. As part of the project,
I will develop and validate a full set of codes using the website OpenCodelist.
Treatment escalation
Escalation to second or third-line immunosuppressive therapies will be assessed through a recorded prescription
for hydroxychloroquine, methotrexate, mycophenolate, azathioprine or leflunomide. The prescription for
adalimumab or infliximab will be measured using the planned link between OpenSAFELY and the targeted
therapies prescribing data.
Corticosteroid burden
Cumulative lifetime corticosteroid doses greater than 500mg have been associated with adverse outcomes in large
nationwide studies of patients with asthma25,26. Cumulative corticosteroid doses will be measured using coding of
GP prescriptions for corticosteroids.
Mortality
All-cause mortality rates will be calculated using OpenSAFELY links to ONS Death Registration Data.
Mortality rates will

secdrp__001_l.text:
be expressed as age-adjusted standardised Mortality Ratios, using indirect standardisation,
according to England mortality data using UK-based life tables as the reference data. Mortality rates will be
compared between groups with different predictors of interest.
Statistical analysis plan
Analyses will be performed to identify factors/predictors associated with the above outcomes including
age, gender, ethnicity, geographical region, socio-economic deprivation, organ involvement and comorbidities.
Justifications for their inclusion are outlined in Table 2.
A competing risk survival model will be performed to estimate the cumulative incidence of each outcome at 1, 2
and 5 years. The start date will be the date of the first recorded diagnosis of sarcoidosis. The end date will be the
time of the outcome of interest. Multiple models will be performed, using the following outcomes of interest:
 Time to death
 Time to end-organ damage, including sub-analyses on organ systems
 Time to treatment with second or third-line immunosuppressive medications
 Time to treatment with a cumulative corticosteroid dose of more than 500mg.
Sample size calculation
I have a fixed population for my st

secdrp__001_m.text:
udy. From my preliminary analyses of data in CPRD, we estimate that 18465
patients were diagnosed with sarcoidosis between 2003 and 2023. CPRD includes GP data from 42 million
patients including many historical patients, this compares to 58 million patients that are currently registered in
OpenSAFELY. Therefore, I estimate that there will be 25750 patients diagnosed with sarcoidosis in OpenSAFELY
between 2004 and 2024.
Using a conservative estimate of 20,000 new diagnoses, a predictive variable that is present in just 10% of the
population but explains an additional 10% increase in the risk of progression to severe disease, gives 93% power
at an alpha of 0.01. This provides confidence that I will be able to detect predictors that are of clinical relevance.
Phase 2 – Quantifying disease pattern and activity on PET-CT using deep-learning techniques (6-18
months)
Overview
Phase 2 will involve the development of a novel AI model to quantify disease burden on 18F-FDG PET-CT scans.
Background
PET-CT in sarcoidosis
18F-FDG PET-CT is a functional imaging modality that is used in the diagnosis and monitoring of patients with
sarcoidosis. It has a high sensitivity to detect active sarcoidosi

secdrp__001_n.text:
s, with estimates ranging between 89% to 100%
across observational studies18.
PET-CT carries significant advantages over non-functional imaging modalities. It can differentiate active
inflammation from “burnt-out” disease. PET-CT can also quantify disease activity by providing objective measures
of 18F-FDG uptake using the Standardised Uptake Values (SUV). Maximum SUV (SUVmax) correlates with
disease activity, including patient-reported symptoms27, FVC18 and cardiac events28,29.
Benefits of AI
AI is being used increasingly for automating time-consuming medical imaging tasks and reducing inter-observer
variability. Convolutional neural network (CNN)-based approaches are a branch of deep learning that are finding
application in the analysis of medical images. CNN approaches are highly effective in imaging tasks such as
recognition, object detection and segmentation and studies suggest that they can reach near-human performance
in well-defined tasks30.
CNNs have proven highly effective in classifying uptake on PET-CT in cancer settings30. Studies have used CNNs
to localise, classify and quantify foci of malignant disease31. In studies in lymphoma patients, CNNs have been
trained to ca

secdrp__001_o.text:
lculate metabolic tumour volume, a value that correlates with progression-free survival31.
Methods
Development of the CNN
A CNN model will be trained to provide a measure of total organ 18F-FDG uptake based on a 3D image. Uptake in
the heart and lungs will be measured as the involvement of these organs is the major cause of death in
sarcoidosis32. Depending on the algorithm’s performance, assessed using test data, measurement of uptake in
other organs will be considered.
PET-CT foci of interest will be detected using segmentation with a fixed thresholding algorithm (e.g. percentage of
local SUVmax or relative uptake compared to the blood pool).
The CNN algorithm will be trained using a historical cohort of PET-CTs performed at KCH in patients with biopsy-
confirmed sarcoidosis. Normal PET-CTs will be used as control images in the validation process. All scans will be
identified from radiology records and anonymised at study inclusion.
Manual segmentation and annotation
Focal areas of increased 18F-FDG uptake will be manually delineated by trained radiologists, using a volume of
interest tool. Identified foci will be as classified as consistent or inconsistent with active sarcoidosi

secdrp__001_p.text:
s. For each focus
of increased 18F-FDG uptake, the classification and anatomical location (i.e. organ) will be recorded. The expert
annotations will allow training of the CNN and testing of sensitivity and specificity for correct classification as
consistent or inconsistent with active sarcoidosis.
CNN training and validation
PET-CTs will be randomly split into three independent subsets; validation (20%), training (60%) and testing (20%).
A validation set will be used to select model hyperparameters and determine the best-trained CNN. The testing
subset will be used to assess the final performance of the model33.
Sample Size Calculation
At KCH, 10-20 PET-CTs are performed in patients with sarcoidosis every month. Using a conservative estimate of
10 PET-CTs per month over the last five years, 600 PET-CT scans will be available to test, train and validate the
algorithm.
Sample size calculations are not routinely performed during the development of deep-learning algorithms. The
inclusion of a higher number of scans is usually associated with improved CNN performance. Sample size will be
limited by the feasibility and time required for manual delineation of FDG activity. We aim to incl

secdrp__001_q.text:
ude 300-500 scans
in the development of the algorithm and believe that this is feasible. This falls in line with similar studies performed
in cancer patients30,34.
Statistical analysis plan
The performance of the automated classification and localisation of 18F-FDG foci will be evaluated by using
sensitivity, specificity and receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves.
If the performance of the segmentation CNN and the classification network is deemed acceptable, defined as
greater than 90% sensitive and specific, I will design a deep learning model capable of quantifying disease activity
in the heart and the lungs. Depending on the performance of the algorithm, uptake in the liver, kidneys, eyes and
lymph nodes may also be measured. A total body disease score will be generated by combining uptake in the
organs of interest.
Phase 3 – Correlating disease activity on PET-CT to disease outcome (12-36 months)
Overview
Phase 3 is an observational prospective study of patients with sarcoidosis with PET-CT performed at baseline.
Using the deep learning model developed in phase 2, I will correlate imaging findings to disease activity at
baseline and one year later.
Background
Although S

secdrp__001_r.text:
UVmax has been shown to correlate with sarcoidosis disease activity, this is poorly characterised and
prospective studies are lacking32. SUVmax is a detail-lacking measurement that does not describe the total
quantity or pattern of uptake. The correlation between objective measurements of PET-CT activity and clinical
outcomes could standardise PET-CT interpretation. This has the potential to standardise clinical trial design and
clinical treatment decisions.
Methods
I will perform an observational prospective study of participants with biopsy-confirmed sarcoidosis with PET-CT
performed at baseline. Clinical outcome markers will be measured at 0- and 12 months post-recruitment.
Participant recruitment
Inclusion criteria
 Biopsy confirmed sarcoidosis.
 Plan for PET-CT under routine NHS care, ensuring no additional radiation exposure on recruitment into
this trial.
Exclusion criteria and justification
 Patients with localised neurosarcoidosis will be excluded from the study as the results will not be
generalisable, due to the limited access to brain-PET-CT imaging.
 Pregnancy or post-partum, due to the harmful risks of radiation during this period.
Recruitment and consent process

secdrp__001_s.text:
Participants will be recruited from KCH. Participants will be identified from sarcoidosis MDTs and be recruited
during routine clinic appointments. Recruitment will adhere to Good Clinical Practice guidelines.
Type and content of participant information materials
Before recruitment, all participants will receive Patient Information Leaflets (PILs). PILs will be co-written with
patient representatives. They will outline what the study involves, including possible risks and what happens after
the study finishes. PILs will explain that recruitment is optional and that consent can be withdrawn at any point.
Equality, Diversity and Inclusion for Study Participants
Participants will be recruited from KCH which serves an ethnically diverse population. Demographic data will be
collected for all patients, including data on protected characteristics. I have created an Equality Impact
Assessment to identify and address factors that may limit the recruitment of underserved populations into the
study. To ensure adequate recruitment of participants from Black Asian and Minority Ethnic (BAME) backgrounds,
I follow the NIHR Toolkit for increasing the participation of BAME groups in healthcare and

secdrp__001_t.text:
social research. I have
also approached the Centre for Ethnic Health Research to provide support in ensuring inclusivity.
Clinical Data
Clinical data will be collected at baseline and 12 months post-PET-CT. Data will include symptom burden, end-
organ damage, hospitalisation and death and is summarised in Table 3.
Study participant support
At the time of recruitment, all participants will receive contact information for the study team (email address and
telephone number). Patient representatives state that study acceptability is high, therefore, we envisage a low
drop-out rate. If participants drop out between the baseline and 12-month visits, their data will be excluded from
the predictive component of the analysis.
Lay summaries of trial results will be provided to all study participants following the completion of the trial.
Sample size calculation
A target sample size of 100 allows for 80% power to detect correlations (or standardised regression coefficients) of
r>.28 at the 5% significance level for organ-specific FDG uptake with disease activity. Furthermore, based on
Green's rule of thumb regression models will contain no more than 6 candidate predictors35.
Statistical analy

secdrp__001_u.text:
sis
Organ-specific FDG uptake will be correlated with disease activity (table 3) at baseline. I will consider including
measures of combined organ uptake, depending on the performance of the algorithm.
Regression models will be used to examine the association between organ-specific FDG-uptake at baseline and
disease activity at 12 months.
A low mortality rate is expected during the period of study follow-up. Therefore, mortality will be reported
descriptively. Depending on mortality and hospitalisation rates, survival analysis will be considered.
Stakeholder meetings:
Stakeholder meetings will take place in all three phases of the project. Patient representatives, Sarcoidosis UK
employees and colleagues from respiratory, cardiology and radiology specialties will be invited. Stakeholders will
be asked to provide feedback on study design and dissemination of study findings. Two meetings will take place in
person and one meeting will take place online.
Dissemination, outputs and anticipated impact
Academic outputs:
I will publish findings from each phase of my research plan in peer-reviewed journals with
high impact factors. I anticipate three key manuscripts; the first, centred on my

secdrp__001_v.text:
epidemiological analyses of
sarcoidosis (Phase 1); the second, detailing the use of deep-learning techniques of PET imaging to quantify
disease activity in sarcoidosis (Phase 2); the third, describing the relationship between sarcoidosis activity on PET-
CT and contemporary disease activity and patient outcome (Phase 3). I will target journals with wide audiences,
ensuring health professionals from multiple clinical specialties can access my research outcomes.
I will present my findings at rheumatology and thoracic conferences. I will use the links that I have built with
cardiology and respiratory colleagues to disseminate findings to a broad range of medical audiences.
Patient outputs:
I have engaged the support of the Sarcoidosis UK Charity as a collaborator to support dissemination strategies to
patients and the wider population. My PPI panel will lead on dissemination strategies that will include:
1. Plain English Summaries to accompany all publications
2. Social media summaries of outputs (including the Facebook Sarcoidosis UK page)
3. Public engagement events (via BSR, EULAR, RCP and Sarcoidosis UK patient council)
4. Newletters via the Sarcoidosis UK charity
Healthcare and

secdrp__001_w.text:
societal outputs:
My research will inform the design of clinical trials of immunosuppressive medications for sarcoidosis on a global
scale. My research will allow non-expert clinicians to identify patients at risk of life-threatening sarcoidosis, who
should be considered for referral to specialist centres and treatment with second-line immunosuppressive
medications. This will improve the care of patients with sarcoidosis, reducing hospitalisations and death.
Impact statement:
My goal is to produce robust and objective methods that are capable of accurately predicting sarcoidosis patients
at risk of severe disease. By identifying high-risk patients, I hope to identify:
 Patients who should be referred to specialist tertiary centres for treatment.
 Patients who will benefit from second-line immunosuppressive therapies, reducing corticosteroid burden
and the risk of flare when steroids are tapered.
 Patients who should be recruited into clinical trials of novel therapies.
By achieving the above, my work will reduce symptom burden and the risk of end-organ damage and mortality. My
work will also drive objective methods of RCT design and inclusion criteria, improving the efficiency o

secdrp__001_x.text:
f research,
which is important for research in a rare condition.
Following my fellowship, I will apply for an NIHR Clinical Lectureship and use my data to inform a nationwide study
of novel treatments for sarcoidosis. In the longer term, the skills that I develop will enable me to reach my goal of
becoming a leading clinical academic in the field of sarcoidosis.
Project management
I have created a research timetable (a Gantt chart), which I will follow during my fellowship.
I will register for a PhD and be supervised according to the KCL PhD supervision structure. This provides a robust
framework for official assessments every 6 months and a formal external peer review at one year.
King’s College London has an excellent academic support structure to facilitate all aspects of my research plan,
including the epidemiological, deep-learning and prospective trial components. The Centre for Rheumatic Disease
is a EULAR Centre of Excellence for rheumatology research. KCH treats a large cohort of sarcoidosis patients,
with monthly multi-disciplinary sarcoidosis meetings. The hospital serves a multicultural and diverse population,
enabling study recruitment with participants from many ethni

secdrp__001_y.text:
cities. The King’s Clinical Trials Unit runs high-quality
clinical trials, including three studies investigating novel therapies in the management of sarcoidosis.
Professor Galloway is a rheumatologist and methodologist, with extensive experience in epidemiological, statistical
and clinical trials-based research and the supervision of PhD students. Within Dr Galloway's research group, I will
have access to a network of qualitative researchers, health psychologists, epidemiologists and statisticians to
support me during my Fellowship.
Dr Katie Bechman is a rheumatologist and methodologist. She has a wealth of experience in epidemiological and
statistical analyses using large health datasets. Dr Bechman and Professor Galloway have an interest in
sarcoidosis and collaborate closely with cardiology, respiratory and radiology colleagues during weekly sarcoidosis
MDTs.
Professor Andrew Reader is a Physicist and Professor in Imaging Sciences. He has extensive experience in the
use of deep-learning techniques in PET-CT imaging. He has a wealth of experience in supervising doctoral
students through the completion of PhDs. He bases his research in the School of Biomedical Engineering and
Ima

secdrp__001_z.text:
ging Sciences, a collaborative centre with plenty of expertise to support the imaging aspects of my project.
During my Fellowship, I will hold monthly meetings with my supervisors and biannual meetings with my
collaborators, to present progress reports. I will work with the KCL finance office to track my budget. I will maintain
a risk register throughout my PhD which will be regularly reviewed with my supervising team.
Ethics
Phase 1
I will undertake epidemiological analyses using OpenSAFELY. No individual data will be visible or transferred to
me. OpenSAFELY adheres to the principles of General Data Protection Regulation (GPRD).
Phase 2
Ethics approval will be obtained for Phase 2 and 3. I will anonymise PET-CTs at study inclusion and remove all
patient-identifiable information.
Phase 3
I will only recruit patients with PET-CT performed during routine clinical care. This will ensure that patients are not
exposed to unnecessary radiation. I have discussed this strategy with patients at the focus group, who agree with
this strategy.
I will apply for ethics approval for Phase 2 and Phase 3 of this project. Before recruitment into this study, written
informed consent will be obtained.

secdrp__001_{.text:
As clinical data will be limited to observational data, I do not envisage any
ethical issues arising from this.
The handling of all data in this project will be performed in compliance with the Caldicott principles and GPRD.
Success criteria
Phase 1:
 Completion of an epidemiology and statistics course
 Presentation of my findings at a national conference
 Publication of a manuscript detailing the demographic and epidemiological risk factors for severe disease
in sarcoidosis.
Phase 2
 Completion of a course in coding, using Python, and in deep learning.
 Presentation of my findings at a national conference
 Publication of a manuscript outlining the deep learning techniques used to quantify and localise disease
activity on PET-CT.
Phase 3:
 Presentation of my findings at an international conference
 Publication of a manuscript detailing the prognostic value of PET-CT in sarcoidosis
I have identified the following risks to delivering this research, with actions to mitigate these.
Phase 1
 Delays in access to OpenSAFELY
o I will engage the support of Brian Mackenna, the direct of NHS Service Analytics, and submit my
data request 6 months before commencing my fellowship.
o I

secdrp__001_|.text:
have costed for the use of CPRD data as a contingency plan as our group already has access to
this. CPRD has a smaller dataset and therefore the use of OpenSAFELY is preferred.
Phase 2
 Inability to develop a sufficiently accurate segmentation CNN and classification network.
If I am unable to develop a tool of sufficient sensitivity and specificity, I will work with a radiology
o
colleague to manually quantify FDG-avidity. I have costed in for the time of a radiologist
throughout Phase 2 and 3 of my study.
 Time-consuming nature of manual segmentation and annotation of PET-CT scans
o After labelling enough scans, I will train a U-net (a CNN tool) to automatically label organ-specific
activity. If the performance of the U-Net is adequate, this will significantly reduce the time needed
for segmentation and annotation.

Phase 3
 Inability to produce a deep learning model capable of measuring PET-CT activity
If Phase 2 fails, I will measure PET-CT activity manually, with the help of a radiology colleague,
o
who has been costed for both Phase 2 and 3.
 Delays in obtaining ethical approval for the observational trial.
o I will submit my ethics application early in the course of my F

secdrp__001_}.text:
ellowship to allow sufficient time to
address feedback.
o I will work with stakeholders (including patients and public representatives) in the design of my
study and ethics submission, increasing the likelihood of a successful outcome.
 Difficulties in patient recruitment and loss of patients to follow-up
o The KCH radiology team perform 10-20 PET-CT scans in patients with sarcoidosis every month.
Therefore, I do not foresee an issue with the availability of PET-CT images to train and validate
the algorithm.
o I will work closely with patient representatives to ensure that the trial is acceptable to patients,
maximising recruitment and minimising drop-out.
o I have already gained support from colleagues in respiratory and cardiology medicine. I will
collaborate with colleagues from other specialities to raise awareness of the trial and increase
recruitment.

sectdars__001_a.text:
Proposed training and development programme
1. Academic training and courses
To advance my knowledge and skills in epidemiological analysis, I will complete the ‘Advanced Course in
Epidemiological Analysis’ hosted by the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. This two-week course
covers a range of epidemiological methodologies in Stata, which will be extremely useful for Phase 1 of my project
and my future research career.
As a KCL student, I will take advantage of the abundance of learning opportunities. To develop skills in
programming, using Python, I will complete the KCL module “Introduction to Programming”. To further my
understanding of deep learning, I will complete the “KCL Masters modules on machine learning and advanced
machine learning”, focusing on the modules in medical imaging.
I will also attend the international “Summer School on Deep Learning for Medical Imaging”. This one-week course
covers the basic and advanced aspects of deep learning, which will prove extremely useful for Phase 2 of my
study.
I will attend the British Medical Association (BMA) Foundation and Advanced Level Leadership Programmes.
These are free to BMA members and cover topics includin
sectdars__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_b.text:
g leadership, personal awareness, resolving conflict and
facilitating collaborative decision making.
1. Conferences and research placement
I will attend conferences in each year of my Fellowship, allowing me to present my research findings and network
with rheumatology and relevant medical specialists. These will include British Society for Rheumatology Annual
Conference (Year 1), the British Thoracic Society (Year 2) and the American College of Rheumatology Annual
Meeting (Year 3).
Through my collaboration with Professor Matthew Baker, I will complete a three-week placement at Stanford
University Medical Centre, within the Sarcoidosis Programme. This is run by a multidisciplinary team, including
specialists in rheumatology, cardiology and respiratory medicine. Through this placement, I will learn from world-
leading academic clinicians and build strong collaborative links that will be extremely useful for my ongoing career.
From a clinical perspective, I will gain clinical knowledge regarding the management of sarcoidosis from the
Sarcoidosis Centre of Excellence, recognised for exceptional patient care. Through my placement, I will gain
exposure to a range of research projects in
sectdars__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_c.text:
sarcoidosis, ranging from bench-to-bedside translational research
projects to clinical trial projects.
I have costed in courses, conferences and a research placement in my application.
2. Formal study
I will enrol in a PhD programme in the School of Immunology and Microbial Sciences at King’s College London. As
part of the Doctoral Training Programme, I will select a range of courses to support my research proposal and my
development as an academic clinician, including: Introduction to Statistics for Researchers, Managing Your
Research Data; Leadership Skills for Research Students; Writing up the Thesis and Preparing for the Viva. I will
also attend courses on using “Stata”.
3. Clinical training and development
Throughout this fellowship, I will attend one NHS clinic per week and one multi-disciplinary meeting per month at
KCH, under the supervision of Professor Galloway. I will rotate through a range of speciality clinics, with a
particular focus on sarcoidosis clinics to complement my research and build upon my clinic knowledge of this
condition. I will also perform one evening of rheumatology on-call duties per week, to maintain my knowledge of
rheumatology emergencies and inpa
sectdars__001_c.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_d.text:
tient management.
During the fellowship, I will maintain an up-to-date clinical portfolio, including a yearly personal development plan
and regular work-based assessments. To do this, I will contribute to my NHS e-portfolio regularly and have bi-
yearly meetings with a clinical supervisor. I will attend Annual Reviews of Competence Progression, ensuring that I
meet and maintain relevant clinical competencies.

techniques in PET-CT imaging. He has a wealth of experience in supervising doctoral students through the
completion of PhDs. He bases his research at the School of Biomedical Engineering and Imaging Sciences, a
collaborative centre with plenty of expertise to support the imaging aspects of my project.
Professor Reader has a wealth of experience in
sectdars__001_e.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_f.text:
medical image reconstruction and analysis in PET-CT imaging.
He has authored over 240 papers in peer-reviewed journals, with a Scopus H-index of 34. He is also the Senior
Editor for the British Journal of Radiology | Artificial Intelligence.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 3
Name Dr Katie Bechman
Institution King's College London
Position Clinical Lecturer in Rheumatology
Description of support provided
Dr Bechman is a Clinical Lecturer at King's College London and a Rheumatology Registrar at King's College
Hospital. She has extensive experience in epidemiology and statistics, making her very well qualified to supervise
Phase 1 of my research project.
Dr Bechman has an impressive publication track record. She has authored over 60 papers in peer-reviewed
journals, with a Scopus H-index of 20. Professor Galloway and Dr Bechman have collaborated on multiple projects
and have an excellent working relationship.
Dr Bechman has a clinical interest in sarcoidosis and collaborates closely with cardiology, respiratory and
radiology colleagues during monthly sarcoidosis MDTs.
Before this application, I worked with Dr Bechman and Professor Galloway on a systematic review of biological
medication
sectdars__001_f.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_g.text:
s in sarcoidosis. We have also recently submitted an abstract to the American College of Rheumatology
on the epidemiology of sarcoidosis in England and are writing a manuscript for submission for publication.
Dr Bechman and Professor Galloway have close collaborative links with the Charity Sarcoidosis UK and have
written several patient information leaflets for their website.
Collaborations
I have engaged the support of medical specialists in the fields of cardiology, respiratory and radiology in this
research proposal.
Professor Surinder Birring is a Consultant Respiratory Physician and Professor of Respiratory Medicine at KCH
and KCL. He has a specialist interest in sarcoidosis, leading the sarcoidosis clinic at KCH and publishing widely in
this area. He is a co-author of the European Respiratory Society guidelines for the treatment of pulmonary
sarcoidosis and developed the Kings Sarcoidosis Questionnaire.
Dr Marko Berovic is a radionucleotide radiologist with expertise in cardiothoracic and musculoskeletal imaging. He
has extensive experience in the use of PET-CT in sarcoidosis and joins the sarcoidosis MDT at KCH monthly.
Dr Daniel Sado is a consultant cardiologist, with a spe
sectdars__001_g.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_h.text:
cialist interest in imaging. He has a particular interest in
heart failure, particularly secondary to sarcoidosis.
Professor Matthew Baker is the Clinical Chief in the Division of Immunology and Rheumatology at Stanford
University and the Co-Director of the Stanford Multidisciplinary Sarcoidosis Program, which is a Centre of
Excellence for the management of sarcoidosis. He has published widely on sarcoidosis.
Host Organisation Support Statement
The following statement of support must be completed by the head of your lead academic department. In order to
contribute to this application, this individual must be invited to do so via the 'Participants and Signatories' section of
the form.
Statement of support
The Department of Inflammation Biology is delighted to support Dr Kathryn Biddle’s application for an NIHR
Doctoral Fellowship. I have read her application and believe it to be novel, well-designed and impactful.
Dr Biddle committed herself to research from an early stage, achieving First-Class Honours in her intercalated
degree in Neurosciences at Cambridge University. She excelled academically during medical school, earning
prizes and graduating with a Distinction. She gained fur
sectdars__001_h.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_i.text:
ther research experience by completing an MSc in
Immunology at the University of Oxford and then pursuing an Academic Clinical Fellowship (ACF) at St George’s
University. She is the first author of five PubMed-cited publications and a co-author of a further three publications.
She has a wealth of experience in communicating her work, through several oral and poster presentations, at
national and international conferences.
Dr Biddle has excelled throughout her clinical and academic career; I believe that she is an excellent candidate for
an NIHR Doctoral Fellowship. She is dedicated, highly motivated and conscientious. Her ability to write this
application whilst juggling full-time clinical duties is a testament to her hard-working and ambitious nature.
Dr Biddle’s research outputs are evidence of her dedication. Despite her ACF being interrupted by the COVID-19
pandemic, she published a research manuscript, several review articles and case reports. She also authored a
book introducing healthcare professionals to research topics.
Alongside her clinical duties and research commitments, Dr Biddle regularly contributes to undergraduate
teaching. She provides introductory lectures on rh
sectdars__001_i.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_j.text:
eumatology and tutorials on inflammatory arthritis, with excellent
feedback. She has also excelled in her leadership roles including the Royal College of Physicians Associate
Clinical Tutor and Chief Rheumatology Registrar.
Dr Biddle developed a keen interest in sarcoidosis during her clinical training. During her involvement with our
department, Dr Biddle has performed a systematic review of the efficacy of biological and targeted synthetic
disease-modifying anti-rheumatic drugs in sarcoidosis. This work has been submitted for publication. She has also
worked with Dr Bechman and Professor Galloway to describe the current epidemiology of sarcoidosis in the UK
using data from Clinical Practice Research Datalink. To develop collaborative relationships, she is organising a
Sarcoidosis Study Day which will be attended by experts from a range of relevant specialities.
KCL will provide robust training oversight for Dr Biddle. She will be based at the Weston Education Centre and
have access to the School of Biomedical Engineering and Imaging Sciences. Her primary supervisor, Professor
Galloway, has a wealth of experience supervising clinical PhD students and receives excellent feedback fr
sectdars__001_j.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_k.text:
om his
supervisees. The success of his mentorship is highlighted by Dr Bechman’s progress from his PhD supervisee to a
NIHR clinical lecturer. She will make an excellent co-supervisor for Dr Biddle’s project.
Professor Galloway’s group is ideally suited to support clinical epidemiology PhDs with excellent dry lab support
and a large group of pre and post-doctoral students who collaborate closely. The group has a very successful
publication record of high-impact systematic reviews and epidemiology studies (including several OpenSAFELY
projects).
Dr Biddle’s final co-supervisor, Professor Reader, has expertise in deep learning in medical imaging. He has
extensive experience in supervising PhD students through to completion and has received wonderful feedback
from his students. Through Professor Reader, Dr Biddle will have access to the School of Medical Imaging. This
will provide an abundance of expertise to guide the deep learning components of her study.
Dr Biddle has established exciting collaborations with clinicians from relevant specialities, including radiology,
respiratory and cardiology medicine. Professor Birring has published widely on sarcoidosis, leads the sarcoidosis
cl
sectdars__001_k.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_l.text:
inic at King’s College Hospital and oversees several clinical trials at the Clinical Trials Unit. Dr Sado provides
expertise in cardiac sarcoidosis and Dr Berovic has a wealth of experience in PET-CT imaging in sarcoidosis.
I fully support Dr Biddle’s plans to undertake a research placement at Stanford University which houses a
Sarcoidosis Centre of Excellence. This will provide an invaluable opportunity to gain clinical and research
experience in sarcoidosis and network with international colleagues.
Dr Biddle’s research proposal has the potential to identify patients at risk of severe forms of sarcoidosis. This
would enable patients to receive earlier treatment with second-line immunosuppressive medications, reduce
corticosteroid-related side effects and improve patient quality of life. Objective methods of PET-CT interpretation
have the potential to drive evidence-based clinical decisions. This study has the potential to change the way that
clinical trials in sarcoidosis are designed and conducted.
In conclusion, the Department of Inflammation Biology offers its unreserved support to Dr Biddle and her
Fellowship Application. The Department is fully committed to ensuring that the
sectdars__001_l.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_m.text:
training, supervision and
infrastructure are in place to facilitate her project. We wish her the best of luck in her application.
